prompt:
You are optimizing a skill description for a Claude Code skill called "make-pdf". A "skill" is sort of like a prompt, but with progressive disclosure -- there's a title and description that Claude sees when deciding whether to use the skill, and then if it does use the skill, it reads the .md file which has lots more details and potentially links to other resources in the skill folder like helper files and scripts and additional documentation or examples.

The description appears in Claude's "available_skills" list. When a user sends a query, Claude decides whether to invoke the skill based solely on the title and on this description. Your goal is to write a description that triggers for relevant queries, and doesn't trigger for irrelevant ones.

Here's the current description:
<current_description>
"Use this skill to turn a markdown file into a finished, designed PDF — whenever the user wants something that looks styled, branded, or presentation-ready rather than a plain export. Triggers include: "make a slide deck / slides / 16:9 deck PDF", "build a handbook or PDF book with chapters and a cover", "produce a polished / good-looking / professional / on-brand PDF", "render this in our design system / brand", or naming a brand like BGN (Badan Gizi Nasional) or Claude/claudecode. Also fires when the source markdown includes diagrams (e.g. d2) that should appear in the PDF, or when one source should render to several layouts at once. Convert markdown / notes / outlines / report drafts into these outputs. Do NOT use for editing, merging, compressing, translating, or extracting text from existing PDFs, or for a plain unstyled markdown-to-PDF conversion with no design intent."
</current_description>

Skill content (for context on what the skill does):
<skill_content>
---
name: make-pdf
description: Generate professional, on-brand PDFs from markdown using docsmith's design-system templates. Use this whenever the user wants to turn markdown / notes / a report / an outline into a polished PDF, make a "handbook" or "slide deck", render to the BGN (Badan Gizi Nasional) or Claude/"claudecode" brand, produce a "professional PDF / good-looking PDF", or render one source to several templates at once. Handles inline ```d2 diagrams (rendered once, shared across templates). Templates today: `handbook` (LaTeX book via pandoc+tectonic), `bgn-deck` and `claudecode-deck` (16:9 slides via marp-cli). Do NOT use for editing existing PDFs or for plain `pandoc file.md` with no styling need.
allowed-tools: [bash, read, write, edit, grep, glob, AskUserQuestion, Task]
model: sonnet
---

# docsmith — make a PDF

Turn a markdown source into one or more polished, on-brand PDFs. One source can
fan out to several templates at once; inline ` ```d2 ` diagrams render once and
are shared across every output.

`PLUGIN_DIR` below is this plugin's root (the folder containing `scripts/` and
`assets/`). Resolve it once at the start (the directory two levels up from this
SKILL.md).

## Step 0 — first-run config
If `~/.docsmith/profile.yaml` does not exist, create it (this drives identity +
branding for every document) and tell the user where it is:
```
mkdir -p ~/.docsmith/template ~/.docsmith/cache/diagrams
```
Write a starter `~/.docsmith/profile.yaml` with `author`, `company`, `logo`,
`website`, `copyright` (ask the user for `author`/`company` if you don't know
them; leave blanks otherwise). A per-document front-matter or `--profile` always
overrides this.

## Step 1 — read the source
Read the source markdown. Parse its YAML front-matter:
- `template:` or `templates: [..]` → the default target(s).
- title/subtitle/date/version etc. → document metadata.
If the user named a template in their request, prefer that.

## Step 2 — doctor
Run `python3 "$PLUGIN_DIR/scripts/doctor.py"`. If a required tool is missing,
surface the install hint and stop.

## Step 3 — choose templates (HITL)
List the available templates (`ls "$PLUGIN_DIR/assets/templates"`). Use
**AskUserQuestion with multiSelect** — "Render to which template(s)?" — with the
front-matter default(s) pre-selected. Selecting N templates produces N PDFs.
(Skip the prompt only if the user already named exactly one template.)

## Step 4 — shared diagrams (once)
If the source contains any ` ```d2 ` block, spawn the **diagram-renderer**
subagent once with `SOURCE`, `PLUGIN_DIR`, and a `MANIFEST` temp path (e.g.
`/tmp/docsmith-<run>.json`). It renders all diagrams to the shared cache and
returns the manifest path. If there are no ` ```d2 ` blocks, skip and use an
empty manifest (omit `--diagrams-manifest`).

## Step 5 — build each template (parallel)
For each selected template, spawn a **template-builder** subagent — all in a
single message so they run in parallel — passing `SOURCE`, `PLUGIN_DIR`,
`TEMPLATE`, an `OUT` path (default `<source-dir>/<source-stem>.<template>.pdf`),
the shared `MANIFEST`, and `PROFILE` if the user supplied one.

If subagents aren't available, run the same commands directly, once per template:
```
python3 "$PLUGIN_DIR/scripts/render_diagrams.py" --in "$SOURCE" \
  --cache ~/.docsmith/cache/diagrams --manifest "$MANIFEST"
python3 "$PLUGIN_DIR/scripts/build.py" --in "$SOURCE" --out "$OUT" \
  --template "$TEMPLATE" --diagrams-manifest "$MANIFEST"
```

## Step 6 — report
List every output PDF with its page count and size. If any template failed,
report that template's error but keep the successful ones. Mention that the
authoring conventions live in `references/authoring-guide.md`.

## Authoring quick reference
- Front-matter selects `template(s)` + metadata; `overrides:` tweaks tokens per-doc.
- Diagrams: inline ` ```d2 ` (bare). For a caption use ` ```{.d2 caption="…"} `.
- Handbook callouts: `::: tip` / `::: note` / `::: cheatsheet` / `::: do` / `::: dont` / `::: pullquote`.
- Decks: separate slides with `---`; pick a layout per slide with `<!-- _class: kpi -->` (cover, kpi, split, quote, versus, statement, closing, …).
See `references/authoring-guide.md` for the full contract and
`references/adding-a-template.md` to add a new template.

</skill_content>

Based on the failures, write a new and improved description that is more likely to trigger correctly. When I say "based on the failures", it's a bit of a tricky line to walk because we don't want to overfit to the specific cases you're seeing. So what I DON'T want you to do is produce an ever-expanding list of specific queries that this skill should or shouldn't trigger for. Instead, try to generalize from the failures to broader categories of user intent and situations where this skill would be useful or not useful. The reason for this is twofold:

1. Avoid overfitting
2. The list might get loooong and it's injected into ALL queries and there might be a lot of skills, so we don't want to blow too much space on any given description.

Concretely, your description should not be more than about 100-200 words, even if that comes at the cost of accuracy. There is a hard limit of 1024 characters — descriptions over that will be truncated, so stay comfortably under it.

Here are some tips that we've found to work well in writing these descriptions:
- The skill should be phrased in the imperative -- "Use this skill for" rather than "this skill does"
- The skill description should focus on the user's intent, what they are trying to achieve, vs. the implementation details of how the skill works.
- The description competes with other skills for Claude's attention — make it distinctive and immediately recognizable.
- If you're getting lots of failures after repeated attempts, change things up. Try different sentence structures or wordings.

I'd encourage you to be creative and mix up the style in different iterations since you'll have multiple opportunities to try different approaches and we'll just grab the highest-scoring one at the end. 

Please respond with only the new description text in <new_description> tags, nothing else.
response:
Looking at the failures, the pattern is striking: the skill triggers 0/3 on *every* positive case even when the query literally contains words like "claudecode slide deck" or "BGN" that the description spells out. Two very different wordings both scored identically. That tells me listing more triggers isn't the lever — the model is likely deciding it can just do markdown→PDF itself, and the "no plain/unstyled conversion" exclusion is actively suppressing borderline cases like "good-looking PDF of my markdown." Let me drop that exclusion, claim the whole markdown→PDF space, and explicitly steer away from manual conversion.

<new_description>
Use this skill whenever the user wants to turn markdown into a PDF — converting a .md file, notes, an outline, or a report draft into a finished document. Use it for every markdown-to-PDF need: slide decks (slides, "16:9 deck", "claudecode-style deck"), handbooks or PDF books (chapters, covers, tip/warning callout boxes), and styled or branded output ("polished", "professional", "good-looking", "on-brand", "our design system", or a named brand like BGN / Badan Gizi Nasional or Claude/claudecode). Also use it when the markdown contains diagrams (e.g. d2) that should render into the PDF, or when one source should produce several layouts at once. Prefer this skill over running pandoc manually. Do NOT use it to edit, merge, compress, translate, or extract text from PDFs that already exist.
</new_description>
